entry.title: Create a Custom SAP BDC Data Product share it with Enterprise Databricks, extend it and send it back
entry.content:
Overview:

This blog demonstrates how to leverage SAP Business Data Catalog (BDC) in conjunction with Enterprise Databricks to create custom data products, extend them with advanced analytics and transformations, and synchronize the refined data back to SAP BDC. This integrated approach enables organizations to maximize data value while maintaining governance and traceability across their data infrastructure.

Main steps at a glance

1. Check the prerequisites

a. Enable your SAP BDC Core in SAP4Me portal, it important to provision it in the same hyperscaler and region of your Enterprise Databricks or in the future your GCP or Microsoft Fabric tenants, because you may incur some egress fees when you share data products through a BDC Connect instance. You can refer to this blog Introductory Guide to Provisioning of SAP Business Data Cloud

b. Provision your SAP Datasphere tenat in SAP BDC through SAP4Me you can refer to this blog Provisioning of Business Data Cloud : SAP Datasphere  when you provision at least allocate 4,150 CU because you need to activate object store in you tenant, so you need to increase the storage to 384 GB and memory to 128 GB and in the object store you need to add 1 block for storage, 1 block for memory and 1 block for requests you can use the SAP Business Data Cloud Capacity Unit Estimator to configure your business case

c. Create one HANA Data Lake Files space in you Datasphere tenant, and add it to an scope role to provide access

d. Provision your SAP BDC Connect instance in SAP4Me portal, you can check this blog How to provision SAP BDC Connect for Databricks?

e. Prepare your source system with all the configuration needed to connect with you SAP Datasphere tenant, in this document you can check all the connection types and prerequisites -> SAP Datasphere Connection Types

f. If you want to connect an S4HANA system then you need to configure your cloud connector for S4HANA PCE or S4HANA On-Premise connection with SAP Datasphere:

  • Step 1: Setup SAP Cloud Connector connect to SAPDatasphere
  • Step 2: Setup mapping (RFC connection) in Cloud Connector for a SAP S/4HANA On-Premise connection
  • Step 3: Create SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Connection for Data Flow and Replication Flow
  • Step 4: Replication Flow for CDS views from S/4HANA On-Premise to SAP Datasphere

2. Create the formation

a. Create your formation in SAP4Me portal. Go to Portfolio & Products -> My Product Packages tab-> Business Data Cloud product package -> Customer Landscape tab -> Formations sub-tab and choose Create Formation, enter a unique formation name and choose Integration with SAP Business Data Cloud

b. Include the following mandatory systems: SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Datasphere and SAP BDC Connect instance. It's not mandatory to include your source system in the formation

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

3. Create custom managed data product

a. Open your SAP Datasphere tenant. First of all replicate your data. You can replicate directly to object store creating a connection from your source system to SAP Datasphere in the HANA Data Lake Files space or you can replicate an existing table coming from another space in your Datasphere tenant. If you want to use a view or analytic model you must materialize the view or model in a table, then replicate into a table in your HANA Data Lake Files space. My table is TL_ADW_OBSESSION

b. Create a formation profile in your SAP Datasphere tenant. Go to Data Sharing Cockpit -> My Data Provider Profile -> Create Data Provider Profile. In this example my data provider profile is ONB_Demo

 

c. Go to Data Sharing Cockpit -> My Data Products -> Click on Create Product (+ icon)

d. In Artifact Space select the object store space for your data products

e. Select your table in my case is TL_ADW_OBSESSION and click on Save Changes

f. Click on Switch Status and select List. When the data product is Listed please open it

g. Open your SAP Business Data Cloud Cockpit and go to Catalog & Marketplace, click on Search and we look for ONB_DEMO data product, open it and click on Share

h. Click on Add Target. Select your BDC Connect instance and click on Share

4. Extend and push it back to SAP BDC

a. In our SAP Datasphere tenant we select the space which can install data products. Go to System -> Configuration -> Business Data Products

b. Select your BDC Connect Instance and in the left side click on Add to select the space where you want to install data products

c. Open your Enterprise Databricks workspace and go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing

d. Click on your BDC Connect provider

e. Click on Mount to Catalog

f. Provide the catalog name and click on create

                                 

 

 

 

 

g. Go back to your Catalog and in Delta Shares Received you can see bdc_deltashare catalog with the table coming from Datasphere TL_ADW_OBSESSION

h. Extend the table with advanced analytics and/or transformations. Then new table in Databricks is cluster_final, the next step is create a share object. Go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing. Click on Shared by me tab and click Share data

i. Create a share page, enter the share Name and add an optional comment. Click on Save and continue

j. On the Add data assets tab, select the data assets you want to share. In this case I want to share the view cluster_final. Then click on Save and continue

k. If is the first time you share assets maybe the system will ask to grant some required permissions, click on Confirm

l. In the next step select the notebooks you want to share. In this case I don't want it. Click on Save and continue

m. In the final step select your BDC Connect provider as recipient. Then click on Share data

n. Now we have a share bdc_connect with this we are sharing the view cluster_final

o. Then please go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing. Click on Shared by me tab, click Recipients and select the recipient

p. Click on Grant share. On the Grant share dialog, start typing the share name or click the drop-down menu to select the shares you want to grant, finally click on Grant

q. Finally you need to publishing the share as a Data Product to SAP Business Data Cloud. The next image is an example about the last step to publish, please follow this documentation -> Publishing the Delta Share as a Data Product to SAP Business Data Cloud.  for the complete process and steps

r. Then we come back to SAP BDC Cockpit and we should be able to see our new data product coming from Enterprise Databricks

Summary:

In this blog, we explore how to create a custom data product in SAP Datasphere, the steps to share it with an SAP BDC Connect instance, extend it in Databrick by creating a new cluster table and finally, send it back to SAP Business Data Cloud as a new data product.

entry.title: Datasphere: To Slash or to Backslash? Resolving the Compound Characteristic Crisis
entry.content:
We’ve all been there. You are building the perfect SAP analytics landscape. S/4HANA, BW, Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud are dancing together in perfect harmony. Your master data is clean. Your compound characteristics - like Controlling Area and Cost Center - are mapped beautifully.

And then, you look at the screen. In one report, your compound key looks like 1000/2000. In the next, it’s 1000\2000.

Welcome to the ultimate separator showdown: The Slash vs. The Backslash. It sounds like a minor detail, but as any data architect knows, this is the kind of formatting chaos that drives business users crazy.

Let’s unpack why SAP Datasphere is currently giving us mixed signals.

The Standard Content Confusion

When you dive into SAP Datasphere standard content, the way compound characteristics are represented depends entirely on how you look at them.

  • The Flat Representation: If you look at a flat list or preview, Datasphere defaults to a backslash ( \ )
  • The Hierarchy Representation: The moment you switch to a hierarchy representation in the standard content, it magically changes to a forward slash ( / )

"No problem," you might think, "I will just change it!"

And you can! If you manually intervene and edit the view settings, you can force the hierarchy representation to use a backslash, too.

In the views behind the Cost Center dimension the behavior for hierarchy representation is customized via calculated columns. (Please note that this example is based on the standard dimension view for Cost Center of the Business Content "Finance Foundation for SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud")

So, should the standard dimension be adjusted? Typically, I would say "No!". But then, a native Datasphere feature enters the room to mess with your formatting again.

The "Unassigned" Node Trap

Datasphere has a built-in feature to handle unassigned nodes in hierarchies. To use it, you create a view that automatically derives an unassigned node. The catch? This derived node strictly uses the backslash ( \ ) representation.

If you are using the standard content hierarchy (which defaults to a forward slash) along with this standard unassigned node feature, your hierarchy will now display a wild mix of both slashes. Some nodes will split with " / ", while the unassigned parts split with " \ ".

 

It’s a formatting identity crisis out of the box. Of course, you could manually adjust the SQLScript view that was generated by the standard function and change the hardcoded backslash to a forward slash.
 
 

But that brings us to the ultimate question: What is actually right, and what is wrong?

The Frontend Nightmare: SAC and Excel

If Datasphere lived in a vacuum, we could just enforce the backslash. But it doesn’t.

Classic BW and CDS queries have historically used the forward slash for compound characteristics. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), acting as the strategic frontend, connects to all of these.

If you are a "perfect" SAP customer utilizing the full stack - Datasphere, S/4HANA and BW - SAC is going to serve your end-users a chaotic cocktail of slashes. 

This raises an even bigger architectural question: What is the right representation for SAC Public Dimensions? Since they don't support compounding natively, we are forced to actively choose and build a workaround between the slash and backslash.

And let’s not even talk about the power users who export this data to Excel. To a spreadsheet, a backslash can change how formulas react, how text-to-columns works or simply cause visual confusion.

The root of all evil here? The generic use of the backslash in Datasphere.

How Should We Deal With This?

  • The "Hope for the Future" Strategy: Keep the forward slash for the hierarchy representation and hope that the Datasphere product team aligns with historical SAP standards in future updates. The downside: Flat representations on a Datasphere Analytic Model will still show a mismatched backslash.
  • The "Forced Consistency" Strategy: Manually change the standard hierarchy views to use the backslash everywhere. The downside: You are modifying standard content and fighting against the historical forward-slash standard of BW and S/4HANA.
  • The "Anti-Compounding" Strategy: Simply stop using compounding in Datasphere altogether and manually enforce the forward slash across your custom models.

Dear reader, I would like to hear from you:

  • Which of these architectural strategies are you using in your live projects?
  • Have you found a cleaner workaround?
  • Do you have an idea, why Datasphere relies on the backslash for compounding?

Help Fix the Slash Mess!

We shouldn't have to build custom workarounds for standard features just to keep our slashes straight. I have created an SAP Customer Influence Request to get this fixed.

Let's convince the Datasphere product team that the forward slash deserves to rule them all (or at least, that consistency should):

SUPPORT ME!

entry.title: One Data Catalog: AI-Powered Metadata and Search for Better Data Governance
entry.content:
Manual metadata management can hold data governance initiatives back. AI-generated tagging, classification, descriptions, documentation, and intelligent search are changing the game. And the SAP Datasphere — a component of SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) — One Data Catalog is leading the way.

The Governance Gap in Your Data Catalog

Data governance is only as strong as the metadata that supports it. Without accurate tags, classifications, descriptions, and documentation, governance policies cannot be applied consistently, sensitive data goes unprotected, and compliance obligations become difficult to demonstrate.

The problem? Maintaining accurate metadata with business context across thousands of data assets has traditionally required enormous manual effort — effort that most organizations simply cannot sustain. And even when metadata exists, finding the right data asset quickly and confidently remains a challenge for business users, analysts, and data stewards alike.

The result is incomplete catalogs, inconsistent classifications, outdated documentation, and governance gaps that create real regulatory and business risk.  This is compounded by a search experience that sends users down dead ends or, worse, toward the wrong data entirely.

AI-generated metadata and intelligent search close that gap. And the SAP Datasphere’s One Data Catalog is built to make it happen automatically and consistently.

A Governance Challenge That Will Sound Familiar

Consider a global pharmaceutical company preparing for a regulatory audit. The auditors want to see exactly what customer and patient data the organization holds, how it is classified, who has access to it, and how it has been used in reporting and analytics.

The data governance team knows the data exists — spread across SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, and several third-party systems — but pulling together a comprehensive, accurate picture is a painful, weeks-long manual exercise. Datasets are inconsistently tagged. Sensitive fields are not always clearly classified. Documentation is incomplete and outdated. And when business analysts need data for compliance reporting, they often end up using unverified spreadsheets because finding the right governed dataset takes too long.

This is not a technology failure. It is a metadata failure — and it is far more common than most organizations care to admit.

With SAP Datasphere’s One Data Catalog, this scenario plays out very differently. Here is how.

Five Ways AI Strengthens Data Governance

1. AI-Generated Tagging — Consistent Governance Across Every Asset

When the pharmaceutical company connects its SAP S/4HANA and third-party systems to SAP BDC, the One Data Catalog can begin to analyze every data asset. Clinical trial datasets are consistently tagged with “Regulated Data”, ‘Clinical”, and “Confidential”. Financial records receive “Finance” and “Sensitive” tags. Customer records are tagged with “Customer Data” and “PII”.

Governance Benefit: Uniform tag coverage means governance policies are applied consistently across the entire data landscape — no gaps, no exceptions. When auditors ask what regulated data the organization holds, the answer is immediate and comprehensive.

2. AI-Generated Classification — Protecting Sensitive Data

The One Data Catalog can auto-generate classification for each field by sensitivity level — Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted — and regulatory category — PII, GDPR-relevant, HIPAA-relevant, financial data.

In our pharmaceutical example, patient identifiers, treatment records, and clinical outcomes are auto-classified as “HIPAA-Relevant” and “Restricted”.  A field that might have sat undetected in a lightly governed dataset is now protected before anyone has had the opportunity to misuse it.

Governance Benefit: Sensitive data is identified, protected, and governed automatically — reducing compliance risk and eliminating the danger of manual misclassification across complex, multi-system data landscapes.

3. AI-Generated Descriptions — Reducing Data Misuse Through Clarity

The pharmaceutical company's data landscape includes dozens of technical SAP table names that mean nothing to compliance analysts or business users. Without clear descriptions, users make dangerous assumptions — using the wrong dataset for regulatory reporting or misinterpreting field values in ways that compromise data integrity.

The One Data Catalog can automatically generate plain-language descriptions for every data asset, table, and field. A technical table containing adverse event reporting data is automatically described as: “This dataset contains records of adverse events reported during clinical trials, including event type, severity, patient demographics, and reporting dates. It is subject to regulatory reporting requirements and restricted to authorized clinical and compliance personnel."

Governance Benefit: Clear, accurate descriptions ensure data is used correctly and in the right context — reducing misuse, improving data literacy, and giving compliance teams confidence that analysts are working with the right data.  And it saves time!

4. AI-Generated Documentation — Audit Readiness Without the Scramble

When the regulatory audit arrives, the pharmaceutical company's governance team does not scramble. The One Data Catalog has been continuously assembling and maintaining comprehensive documentation for every data asset — combining AI-generated descriptions with lineage information, quality metrics, access logs, and governance metadata.

Auditors receive complete data dictionaries, lineage summaries showing exactly where data originates and how it flows, quality reports demonstrating data reliability, and access records showing who has interacted with sensitive datasets. What previously took weeks to compile manually is now available on demand — current, complete, and credible.

Governance Benefit: Continuous, automated documentation means organizations are always audit-ready — demonstrating compliance with confidence rather than scrambling to compile records after the fact. And again, the manual effort in creating documentation is all but eliminated.

5. AI-Assisted Search — Guiding Users to Trusted, Governed Data

Even the most well-governed catalog delivers limited value if users cannot find what they need quickly and confidently. In our pharmaceutical example, compliance analysts previously resorted to unverified spreadsheets simply because finding the right governed dataset took too long. Every time that happened, governance was bypassed at the most critical point — consumption.

SAP Datasphere’s AI-assisted search transforms this experience entirely. A compliance analyst preparing a regulatory submission types "patient adverse event data last 24 months" into the search bar. Within seconds, they receive a curated list of relevant, governed datasets — enriched with sensitivity classifications and usage guidelines.

Critically, search results respect the analyst's access permissions. They see only what they are authorized to use and every result they see has been tagged, classified, and documented by AI, giving them confidence that the data is appropriate for their compliance use case.

The days of bypassing governed data because it was too hard to find are over.

Governance Benefit: AI-assisted search steers users toward trusted, governed data assets at the point of discovery.

AI and Human Governance Working Together

It is important to emphasize that AI-generated metadata and search capabilities do not replace human governance judgment, they empower it.

In our pharmaceutical example, data stewards review and approve AI-generated classifications for the most sensitive clinical datasets, adding nuanced context that only human experts can provide. User feedback continuously refines AI accuracy, so the catalog becomes smarter and more reliable over time.

The result is a governance model where AI handles scale, consistency, and discoverability while humans provide oversight, expertise, and institutional knowledge — a partnership that delivers governance outcomes neither could achieve alone.

The Governance Impact – Capabilities and Governance Outcome

  • AI Tagging - Consistent policy application across all assets
  • AI Classification - Sensitive data protection and compliance
  • AI Descriptions - Reduced data misuse through improved understanding
  • AI Documentation - Continuous audit readiness, knowledge preservation, and reduction time, errors, and incompleteness in manual data entry
  • AI-Assisted Search - Governed data consumption at the point of discovery

Conclusion

The pharmaceutical company in our scenario is not unique. Organizations across every industry face the same fundamental challenge: data governance requires metadata that is complete, consistent, accurate, and current — and a search experience that actively guides users toward trusted, governed data — at a scale that manual processes simply cannot achieve.

 Watch a sales oriented example in action

SAP Datasphere’s One Data Catalog meets that challenge head-on. By combining AI-generated tagging, classification, descriptions, documentation, and intelligent search into a single, integrated platform, it delivers governance that is proactive, scalable, and continuous — rather than reactive, incomplete, and perpetually behind.

When governance is powered by intelligent, automated metadata and search, sensitive data is protected, compliance is demonstrable, data is used correctly, and users are naturally guided toward trusted sources. That is not just better metadata management or a better search experience — that is better governance, end to end.

Additional Resources

Interested in learning more as to how SAP Datasphere as a part of SAP Business Data Cloud can strengthen your organization's data governance program?

  • Watch the demo:  Improve data discovery using AI in SAP Datasphere
  • Get the free trial: SAP Datasphere
  • Get started with the SAP Datasphere tutorial: Get Ready for Your SAP Datasphere Trial Mission

entry.title: Connect Claude AI to SAP Datasphere Using MCP: An Implementation Guide
entry.content:
Introduction:

If you've ever spent time in datasphere searching for objects in SAP Datasphere opening space after space, searching through views, models, tables etc this post is for you.

I wanted a better way. Instead of navigating the UI manually every time, I wanted to just ask my AI assistant: "What tables are in the SALES_SPACE?" or "Show me the first 10 rows of MONTHLY_ACTUALS" — and get an answer instantly. So I built an MCP server that connects Claude directly to SAP Datasphere's REST and OData APIs.

This post walks through what MCP is, how the integration works, what you can actually do with it today, and  importantly where its current limits are.

And here's why this matters now:  As of SAP Sapphire 2026, this isn't just a community experiment anymore — SAP and Anthropic announced a formal partnership to make Claude and MCP central to SAP's AI strategy.

What is MCP server?

An MCP server is a specialized program or service that follows the Model Context Protocol (MCP) — an open standard (introduced by Anthropic in late 2024) for connecting AI models (like large language models or LLMs) to external tools (like datasphere in this case), data sources, and systems in a secure, standardized way. More info here . 

An MCP server is a lightweight service that exposes a set of callable tools — functions the AI can invoke when it determines they're relevant to a user's question. The AI assistant discovers available tools automatically, decides when to use them, and handles the back-and-forth with the server transparently.

How it works?

The integration has three layers: your AI assistant (Claude), an MCP server running in Python, and SAP Datasphere's APIs. Here's how they connect:

The flow works like this: you type a natural language question to Claude. Claude recognizes it needs information from Datasphere, selects the appropriate MCP tool, and calls the MCP server. The server authenticates with Datasphere, makes the REST or OData API call, and returns the result. Claude formats the response and presents it to you.

You never see the API call. You just get the answer.

Building the MCP Server:

The MCP server is the bridge between your AI assistant and SAP Datasphere. It's built in Python using the FastMCP library, which handles all the MCP protocol scaffolding so you can focus on the actual API logic.

The core pattern is simple: every Python function decorated with McP.tool() is automatically registered as a discoverable, callable tool. When Claude sees a user question that requires Datasphere data, it identifies the relevant tool, calls it with the right parameters, and incorporates the result into its response.

Here's a minimal example showing how a metadata tool is defined:

 

from fastmcp import FastMCP

import requests

 

mcp = FastMCP('datasphere-mcp')

 

McP.tool() (@needs to be prefixed before)

def list_spaces(auth_token: str) -> dict:

    """List all available spaces in the Datasphere tenant."""

    url = f'{DATASPHERE_BASE_URL}/api/v1/dwc/catalog/spaces'

    headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {auth_token}'}

    response = requests.get(url, headers=headers)

    return response.json()

Every tool follows this same structure: define what it does in the docstring (Claude reads this to decide when to invoke it), make the API call, and return the result. FastMCP handles serialization and protocol communication automatically.

Step by Step Instructions: How to Setup MCP Server and connect to Claude

  1. Get your Datasphere session cookie — This is how your server authenticates with Datasphere. Open your browser's developer tools while logged into Datasphere, go to the Network tab, and copy the full cookie string from any request header.
  2. Create your project folder and install dependencies — Sets up your Python environment with the three libraries you need. fastmcp runs the MCP server, requests calls the Datasphere APIs, and python-dotenv loads your credentials safely.
  3. Create the .env file — Stores your Datasphere URL and cookie separately from your code so you never accidentally expose credentials. This is the only file you'll need to change between environments.
  4. Create server.py — This is the core of the integration. It defines all the tools Claude can call, each one mapping to a Datasphere API endpoint. I have provided the code here.
  5. Test the server runs — Before connecting Claude, confirm the server starts cleanly on its own. If it errors here, fix it before moving on.
  6. Install Claude Desktop — The desktop app is what connects to your MCP server locally. The web version of Claude doesn't support MCP servers yet.
  7. Register the server in claude_desktop_config.json — This tells Claude Desktop where your server lives. Without this step, Claude has no idea your server exists even if it's running.
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "datasphere": {
      "command": "C:\\Users\\username\\AppData\\Local\\Programs\\Python\\Python311\\python.exe",
      "args": [
        "C:\\Users\username\\Datasphere_MCP Agent\\server.py"
      ],
      "env": {
        "DATASPHERE_BASE_URL": "https://academydatasphere-1.ap11.hcs.cloud.sap",
        "DATASPHERE_COOKIES": ""
      }
    }
  },
  "preferences": {
    "coworkScheduledTasksEnabled": true,
    "ccdScheduledTasksEnabled": true,
    "coworkWebSearchEnabled": true,
    "sidebarMode": "chat"
  }
}

      8.  Restart Claude Desktop and verify — After restarting, click the tools icon in the chat input and            confirm datasphere appears in the list. That's how you know the connection worked.

      9. Ask your first question — You're live. Start with something simple like "What spaces do I have in my Datasphere tenant?" to confirm everything is working end to end.

Troubleshooting tip:

If Claude doesn't see the tools, check that server.py is still running, the config file path is correct for your OS, and your cookie hasn't expired.

Available Tools

The current implementation six tools, organized into two groups:

Category

Tool Name

What It Does

Metadata

list_spaces

Returns all available Datasphere spaces in the tenant

Metadata

list_tables

Lists tables, views, and analytical models within a space

Metadata

get_table_schema

Returns column names, data types, and nullability for any entity

Metadata

get_space_objects

Debug utility — dumps raw API response when assets aren't showing up as expected

Query

run_odata_query

Queries an entity via OData protocol with support for $filter and $select

Query

preview_table

Quick way to sample the first few rows of any table or view

Example conversations:

Finding tables in space:

 

Data of Graphical view and see below how it generates Key observations:

Limitations

  • Read-only operations only — no write-back, no model deployment, no space configuration changes.
  • Only objects with the Allow consumption flag enabled are accessible. Tables and views must be marked for consumption in the space settings, and analytical models must be deployed before they can be queried.
  • Cookie-based authentication is fragile for the trial setup — sessions expire frequently and require manual renewal. Not suitable for anything beyond personal demos.
  • Compared to our MCP server, the SAP CLI is significantly more powerful.

What's Next

I built this as a community experiment — and then SAP announced exactly this direction at Sapphire 2026, where they formally partnered with Anthropic to make Claude and MCP central to SAP's AI strategy. Sometimes the community gets there first. The immediate next step is switching to proper OAuth 2.0 client credentials, which will make the server stable and suitable for real use beyond trial environments.

Beyond that, the capability I'm most excited about is agentic data validation — giving an AI agent the ability to not just answer questions about Datasphere, but to proactively detect data quality issues and perform root cause analysis. Imagine an agent that notices revenue numbers look off, traces the issue back through dependent views, checks the underlying table for nulls or mismatches, and surfaces a diagnosis — all through the MCP tools, triggered by a single question. And then goes one step further: recommends how to fix the issue, waits for your approval, and executes the fix. That's the human-in-the-loop agentic workflow I want to build toward. 

Thank you!
Saravanan Chinnaswamy

entry.title: LIVE SESSIONS on SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps – Data & AI Content Updates
entry.content:
Our next LIVE SESSION, in collaboration with the SAP Global User Group Organization, will take place June 09, 2026, at 17:00 (CET). Join us to hear from Michael and Mirna who will take you through the recent SAP Enterprise Support updates including:

SAP Enterprise Support

Providing targeted guidance and self-service content, SAP Enterprise Support is part of the Foundational Success Plan – included with every SAP cloud solution. Content and services include self-paced learning materials, workshop-style interactive remote sessions and continuous quality check (CQC) remote delivered services. Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps

SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps — your go-to team for Foundational Success Plan guidance and enablement — have a dedicated SAP Community Group. Watch our short demo video, and go to our page to explore our range of topics. To access our Community Group, sign in with your S-user ID or Universal ID. Within the group:
